title: Rethinking the C-Suite & Using Copycats to Your Advantage
content:
Poorly designed executive teams can severely slow decisions, confuse accountability, and drain a company's energy. Rather than functioning as mechanisms of execution, these teams often devolve into mere forums for status updates and political positioning. This dynamic leaves CEOs wondering why a group of outstanding individual leaders consistently fails to perform as a collective unit. The erosion of the C-suite into an exclusive club is largely driven by rampant 'chief inflation' over the past two decades. Today, companies frequently hand out 'chief' titles as status markers or recruiting perks, leading to bloated meetings where priorities blur.

Larger executive teams naturally invite 'social loafing,' allowing individuals to hide within a group and exert less effort. This results in diffuse accountability and sluggish decision-making that hampers the entire organization. Designing the executive team is arguably one of the most consequential choices a CEO can make to determine how an enterprise allocates resources. Unfortunately, team composition is often shaped by vanity titles and inherited structures rather than deliberate strategy. To drive real execution, CEOs must purposefully align their executive team's design with their company's core strategic goals.
